4 min read
Just now

--

There was a time when buying something meant owning it.
A book sat on your shelf for decades. A tractor could be repaired by a local mechanic. A television lasted a generation. A car was more than transportation – it was a machine you could understand, modify, and pass on.
Ownership implied control.
Today, that definition is quietly changing.
Across industries, we are witnessing a subtle but profound shift: from ownership to permission.
Increasingly, we do not own products in the traditional sense. We purchase access to ecosystems governed by software, cloud services, and digital platforms. The product may sit in our home or garage, but much of its functionality, evolution, and even longevity is determined elsewhere.
This transformation did not begin with automobiles.
It began with software.
Applications that were once purchased outright became subscriptions. Music collections gave way to streaming platforms. Films moved from DVDs to digital libraries. Games became online services. Artificial intelligence is increasingly delivered through cloud platforms rather than software installed on personal computers.
The business model changed because it offered companies something incredibly valuable: recurring revenue, continuous customer relationships, and the ability to improve products after purchase.
Now that same philosophy is extending into the physical world.
A smartphone is no longer just a communication device. It has become the command center for payments, health, entertainment, navigation, identity, and increasingly, the connected home.
Smart televisions receive new features long after they are sold. Air conditioners can be managed remotely. Security cameras upload footage to the cloud. Appliances communicate with mobile applications. Vehicles receive software updates overnight.
The Internet of Things is creating a world where individual products no longer exist in isolation. They exist as members of larger ecosystems.
This delivers remarkable convenience.
Devices work together seamlessly. Experiences become more personalized. Maintenance can become predictive rather than reactive. Energy consumption can be optimized. Software can fix problems that once required physical intervention.
These are genuine advances.
But every technological shift introduces new trade-offs.
As ecosystems become more integrated, switching between them can become more difficult. Repair increasingly depends on proprietary diagnostics and authorized service networks. Software determines which features are available, when they are updated, and in some cases, whether they continue to function at all.
The question is no longer whether a product works.
The question is: who ultimately controls it after it has been purchased?
This debate is becoming particularly relevant in the automotive industry.
Software-defined vehicles are transforming the automobile from a mechanical asset into a digital platform. Features are activated through software, batteries are becoming structural components, and over-the-air updates continuously reshape the ownership experience.
Some manufacturers are pursuing highly integrated architectures that maximize efficiency and performance. Others continue to emphasize modularity, repairability, and long-term serviceability.
Neither approach is inherently right or wrong.
One prioritizes optimization.
The other prioritizes resilience.
This is not merely an engineering decision. It is a philosophical one.
History often oscillates between openness and consolidation.
The personal computer encouraged openness. The smartphone era concentrated power within a handful of digital ecosystems. Artificial intelligence is now entering a similar phase, where a relatively small number of companies possess the infrastructure, computing power, and data required to build frontier models.
The same questions are beginning to emerge across mobility, healthcare, manufacturing, finance, and agriculture.
Who owns the data?
Who controls updates?
Who decides compatibility?
Who determines when a product reaches the end of its useful life?
For countries like India, these questions extend beyond consumer preference. They touch industrial policy, technological capability, digital sovereignty, and long-term economic resilience.
India has an opportunity not merely to manufacture products, but to shape the principles on which future ecosystems are built.
Can technology remain connected without becoming unnecessarily restrictive?
Can innovation coexist with repairability.
Can software-driven products preserve meaningful consumer choice?
These are not questions for engineers alone. They concern policymakers, businesses, investors, and consumers alike.
The next decade will not simply determine which companies build the best products.
It will determine the very meaning of ownership in a connected world.
Perhaps the defining question of the AI and IoT era is not whether our devices become smarter.
It is whether we remain their owners – or gradually become users within ecosystems we no longer control.
